data.title: Dr. Reddy’s biosimilar for Bristol’s Orencia undergoes FDA review
data.description:
Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories (RDY) announced on Friday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has accepted for review its marketing application for a biosimilar targeting Orencia, a blockbuster arthritis therapy marketed by Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY).

If approved, the product named DRL_AB will be available as an intravenous infusion for adults with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriatic arthritis, the company said.

The Indian drugmaker had submitted the 351 ((k)) Biologics License Application for DRL_AB in December, becoming the first to pursue U.S. approval for an off-patent version of Orencia (abatacept).

The company seeks interchangeable status for the drug, meaning if approved, DRL_AB could be substituted at the pharmacy level without the intervention of the healthcare provider who first prescribed the reference product.

In 2025, Bristol-Myers (BMY) generated $2.7B in revenue from Orencia in the U.S., indicating a ~1% YoY decline as lower selling prices partly offset higher demand.

data.title: Western Union stock drops after Q4 revenue disappoints
data.description:
Western Union (WU) stock fell 6.1% in Friday morning trading after the company's Q4 revenue fell short of consensus, even though its 2026 earnings guidance was largely in line with Wall Street expectations.

The company known for wire transfers of cash expects 2026 adjusted EPS  of $1.75-$1.85 (midpoint $1.80 vs. $1.79 consensus) and full-year revenue growth of 5%-8%, implying $4.25B-$4.37B (midpoint $4.31B vs. the $4.12B consensus).

Q4 adjusted EPS of $0.45, beating the average analyst estimate of $0.43, fell from $0.47 in Q3 and increased from $0.40 in Q4 2024. 

Q4 revenue of $1.01B, trailing the $1.04B consensus, decreased from $1.03B in the prior quarter and $1.06B a year ago. Q4 adjusted revenue, excluding Iraq, was $1.00B in Q4 2025, down from $1.02B in Q3 and $1.03B in Q4 2024.

"Despite a challenging operating environment in 2025, we delivered meaningful progress across the business," said President and CEO Devin McGranahan. "We strengthened our Consumer Services offerings, expanded our owned retail footprint, and accelerated our transition to a more digital-first operating model."

Consumer money transfer revenue of $871.5M fell 7% Y/Y, while consumer services revenue of $136.9M increased 15%. Branded digital revenue, which makes up 30% of consumer money transfer revenue, increased 7% Y/Y on a reported basis.

Western Union's (WU) consumer services business's growth reflects the expansion of its Travel Money business, which included the acquisition of Eurochange Limited, and higher revenues from the company's bill pay business.

data.title: First Solar surges after Supreme Court strikes down Trump's 'reciprocal' tariffs
data.description:
First Solar (FSLR) +5.1% in Friday's trading after the U.S. Supreme Court ruled IEEPA "reciprocal" tariffs are illegal; starting in February 2025, President Trump used the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to implement tariffs on essentially all U.S. imports.

Wells Fargo analysts said First Solar (FSLR) is one of the biggest beneficiaries of the ruling, as the company is helped by lower duties on imported unfinished goods for its U.S. finishing line, which also allows the company to redirect India-made production to the U.S. at higher ASPs; as somewhat of an offset, broader U.S. panel prices could dip slightly, pressuring ASPs.

Wells Fargo said several other clean energy firms should benefit, with as much as 300 bps of margin exposure, including Enphase Energy (ENPH), Nextpower (NXT), Shoals Technologies (SHLS), and Array Technologies (ARRY): shares are up 3.4%, 3%, 2.9%, and 2.1%, respectively.

ETF: (TAN)

data.title: Here are other laws Trump can use to justify tariffs
data.description:
The Supreme Court's decision that it's not within President Donald Trump's authority to impose sweeping tariffs under the International Economic Emergency Powers Act ((IEEPA)) doesn't mean that his ability to impose tariffs will be restricted significantly.

Even before Friday's ruling, administration officials have said they'll look for alternative ways to impose tariffs if the Supreme Court rejected Trump's IEEPA tariffs.

Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh spells out in his dissent a range of other laws that the administration can turn to to apply such taxes. 

The Trade Act of 1974

Section 122: "permits the President to impose a 'temporary import surcharge' to 'deal with large and serious United States balance-of-payments deficits.'"

Section 201 allows the president to take "appropriate and feasible action," including duties, if the International Trade Commission rules that a specific item is being imported in such large amounts that it causes a “serious injury” or threat to domestic production.

Section 301 allows duties if the president determines "an act, policy, or practice of a foreign country" is "unjustifiable and burdens or restricts United States commerce."

Tariff Act of 1930

Section 338 allows the president to impose tariffs when he determines that "any foreign country places any burden or disadvantage upon the commerce of the United States."

Trade Expansion Act of 1962

Section 232 gives the president the power to "adjust the imports of [an] article and its derivatives so that such imports will not threaten to impair the national security" if the Secretary of Commerce provides a report indicating that need.

According to reporting by Bloomberg News, many of the laws do have limits in terms of amount and their duration. While the Section 232 law has no limits on the tariff rate of duration, the Section 122 regulation puts a 15% cap on the tariff rate and a limit of 150 days, which can be extended with Congressional approval.

Section 201 tariffs have a four-year initial term, which can be extended to a maximum of eight years. Its rate limit is 50%, which must be phased down after a year.

The Section 301 tariffs also have an initial term of four years but can be extended with no maximum limit, Bloomberg said.

"So the Court’s decision is not likely to greatly restrict Presidential tariff authority going forward," Kavanaugh concluded. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Retail rejoices: The NRF approves the Supreme Court ruling on tariffs
data.description:
The National Retail Federation issued a statement in response to the Supreme Court’s ruling regarding the Trump administration’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose tariffs.

The retail sector group said the Supreme Court’s announcement regarding tariffs provides much-needed certainty for U.S. businesses and manufacturers, enabling global supply chains to operate without ambiguity. The NRF thinks that clear and consistent trade policy is essential for economic growth, creating jobs and opportunities for American families. Looking ahead, the NRF urged the lower court to ensure a seamless process to refund the tariffs to U.S. importers. The NRF's view is that the refunds will serve as an economic boost and allow companies to reinvest in their operations, employees, and customers.

Notable tariff decision gainers included JAAKS Pacific (JAKK) +26%, Floor & Decor (FND) +5.6%, Etsy (ETSY) +5.0%, Wayfair (W) +4.4%, and Stellantis (STLA) +3.8%. 

Amazon (AMZN) was up 2.4% following the SCOTUS ruling.

Many retailers called out the impact of tariffs on margins in their fourth-quarter earnings reports, although productivity efficiency programs, higher pricing to customers, and cost streamlining were used as offsets.

data.title: AbbVie wins FDA nod for Venclexta-acalabrutinib combo in first-line chronic lymphocytic leukemia
data.description:
AbbVie (ABBV) said Friday that the U.S. FDA has approved a supplemental new drug application for its combination regimen of Venclexta (venetoclax) and acalabrutinib to treat previously untreated adults with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL).

The Illinois-based drugmaker markets Venclexta with Roche (RHHBF) (RHHBY). Acalabrutinib is a Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitor marketed by AstraZeneca (AZN) as Calquence. The approval is supported by data from the Phase 3 AMPLIFY trial.

This milestone updates the treatment of CLL in the first-line setting, establishing the Venclexta and acalabrutinib combination as the first and only all-oral, fixed-duration regimen for previously untreated patients, the company said.

The regimen supports current standards of care by offering patients the potential for time off treatment and giving providers a new, targeted option that combines two classes of oral medications for CLL.

"This FDA approval marks a significant milestone for AbbVie and, more importantly, for people living with CLL," said Svetlana Kobina, vice president, global medical affairs, oncology, AbbVie.

"As the first and only all-oral, fixed-duration combination regimen for previously untreated patients, the Venclexta plus acalabrutinib approval expands choice and flexibility for patients and providers navigating complex treatment decisions in CLL."

Update: 11.11 AM ET: Adds other relevant tickers.

data.title: CarGurus' Q4 sales top expectations, sets upbeat outlook on bullish market conditions
data.description:
With the help of AI-driven products, additional dealers, and expanding wallet share, CarGurus (CARG) realized higher sales and increased profits in the fourth quarter, driving shares higher at the open on Friday.

The results, coupled with expectations for favorable market trends and industry conditions, led the company to issue first quarter and full year sales guidance that was better than Wall Street anticipated.

CarGurus (CARG) earned an adjusted profit of $61.0M in Q4, or $0.63 per share on an adjusted basis, an increase of 17% from the same quarter last year and in-line with expectations, while a key operating income measure increased by 13% to $88.5M.

Driven by a 7% increase in total paying dealers (U.S. and international) and 8% increase in quarterly average revenue per subscribing dealer ((QARSD)), total sales increased by 15% during the quarter to $241.1M, exceeding estimates by more than $2M.

As for the company’s outlook for the current quarter, CarGurus (CARG) expects to earn an adjusted profit between $0.52 and $0.58 per share on $240.5M and $245.5M in sales. This compares to the consensus estimate of $0.57 per share on $238.8M in sales.

For FY26, revenue is expected to increase by 10% to 13%, translating to a range of $997.7M and $1.02B versus $993.98M estimates.

In the wake of Q4 results and upbeat guidance, shares of CarGurus (CARG) are higher for a fifth consecutive day and expected to end a five-week losing streak.

data.title: U.S. video game sales up 3% in January, led by content, Switch 2 spending
data.description:
Total U.S. video game sales rose 3% for the month of January from last year, according to a research report from Circana on Friday.

January sales came in at $4.70B compared with $4.56B for the same period last year.

The bulk of sales, as usual, was generated by game content, which rose 3% to $4.27B for the month. The growth was led by a 23% increase in subscription spending combined with slight gains across console and PC content, which offset a small drop in mobile content.

Hardware sales rose 16% to $248M for the month, while accessory sales saw a 5% decline from the prior year to $185M.

When looking closely at hardware numbers, Circana analyst Mat Piscatella noted that Nintendo Switch 2 spending was able to offset year-on-year declines seen across PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, and Switch.

In content, Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 repeated as the best-selling game of the month, the report said. Code Vein II was the only new release to appear on the January best-selling titles chart, ranking 11th overall. It came first on the PC aggregated storefronts chart.

Final Fantasy VII: Remake jumped to 9th in January from 225th in December benefitting from the release of the Switch 2 and Xbox Series versions of the game during the month, the report said.

The month's top 10 mobile games by U.S. consumer spending are MONOPOLY GO!, Royal Match, Last War: Survival, Candy Crush Saga (NASDAQ:MSFT), Gossip Harbor, Kingshot, Whiteout Survival, Royal Kingdom, Township, and Free Fire.

"The Clash Royale show seems to be officially over, as the game falls out of the top 10 ranking with -49% revenue over Dec 2025," said Sam Aune, the gaming insight analyst at Sensor Tower. "The game to keep your eye on is Loom Games' Pixel Flow!, which grew IAP revenue 79% month over month."

Related tickers: OTCPK:NTDOY, SONY, MSFT, TBCH, LOGI, CRSR, EA, TTWO, WBD, OTCPK:UBSFY, OTCPK:NCBDY, OTCPK:SQNNY, OTCPK:CCOEY, OTCPK:SGAMY, OTCPK:KONMY, OTCPK:NEXOY, SKLZ, SE, NTES, OTCPK:TCEHY, RBLX.

Retail stock: GME

ETFs: GAMR, ESPO, NERD

data.title: ‘The policy backdrop is just so much more supportive for growth this year’ – BofA’s senior economist
data.description:
The U.S. economy could achieve 3% growth this year as the policy environment shifts dramatically in favor of expansion, according to Aditya Bhave, senior U.S. economist at Bank of America.

“The policy backdrop is just so much more supportive for growth this year,” Bhave said in an interview with CNBC, pointing to easier fiscal policy, a Federal Reserve that has cut rates to neutral or possibly accommodative levels, and reduced trade uncertainty compared to last year.

While fourth-quarter GDP figures appeared soft, Bhave emphasized that the government shutdown skewed the results significantly. The shutdown alone accounted for 1.1 to 1.2 percentage points of lost growth.

“Ex of that, you were at 2.5-2.6 [percentage points]. That’s totally fine in terms of GDP,” Bhave explained, noting that consumer momentum remained solid heading into the first quarter.

Inflation remains a concern, with tariffs playing a notable role in elevated price readings. Bhave estimated that tariffs currently account for approximately 40 to 50 basis points of core PCE inflation. Without the tariff impact, inflation would be running at 2.5% to 2.6%— “better than 3%, obviously, but it’s still quite a bit above what the Fed targets,” he noted.

Three key factors are driving the improved outlook for 2026: fiscal stimulus hitting the economy imminently, the Fed’s rate cuts to neutral territory, and the absence of major trade uncertainty shocks. Bhave suggested the economy could easily surpass 2.5% growth for the full year, with AI investment serving as a primary driver throughout 2026.

The consumer picture, however, reveals a more complicated story beneath the positive aggregate data. Bhave characterized spending patterns as “k-shaped,” with Bank of America’s internal credit and debit card data showing strong acceleration among higher-income households while lower-income cohorts continue to struggle.

“When I say the consumer’s fine, that’s definitely a comment about kind of aggregates averages because that’s what we deal with in macro,” Bhave acknowledged.

On potential growth, Bhave pushed back against the Fed’s estimate of 1.8%, arguing that the economy’s true capacity is closer to 2%-2.5%.

He pointed to continued solid investment growth expectations and noted that while AI will drive near-term gains, broader investment themes like domestic factory construction represent longer-term opportunities for the economy.

More on U.S. economy

  • 2025 real GDP growth was the softest in almost a decade, excluding COVID-19
  • U.S. GDP rises by 1.4% in Q4, below consensus, in BEA's advance estimate
  • Fears of inflation and collapsing growth ‘have not played out the way many thought’ – Jefferies’s David Zervos
  • Atlanta Fed GDPNow revises Q4 GDP growth downward to 3.0%
  • U.S. trade deficit in 2025 stands at $901B
